class_schedule.topic: Incident Response Framework and Team Structure
learning_objectives: Describe the NIST incident response lifecycle
Define roles and responsibilities in a CSIRT
readings: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 Computer Security Incident Handling Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: When a security incident occurs, the difference between a minor disruption and a catastrophic breach often comes down to the quality of the incident response plan and the team executing it.
key_concepts: NIST IR lifecycle: Preparation, Detection & Analysis, Containment, Eradication & Recovery, Post-Incident
CSIRT roles: IR manager, triage analyst, forensic analyst, threat intelligence, communications
Incident classification and severity levels
IR plan components: scope, authority, escalation paths, communication templates
Tabletop exercises and IR readiness assessment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a real-world incident response plan and conducting a mini tabletop exercise with a ransomware scenario

review.value: Cyclical process: Preparation ensures readiness; Detection identifies incidents; Containment limits damage; Eradication removes the threat; Recovery restores operations; Post-Incident improves future response.
Typically 4 levels: Critical (business-threatening), High (significant impact), Medium (moderate impact), Low (minimal impact). Severity drives response urgency and escalation.

class_schedule.topic: Detection and Analysis: SIEM and Threat Hunting
learning_objectives: Configure SIEM detection rules for common attack patterns
readings: Blue Team Handbook: SOC, SIEM, and Threat Hunting Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Detection is the first step of incident response. Modern SOCs combine automated SIEM rules with proactive threat hunting to find adversaries before they achieve their objectives.
key_concepts: SIEM architecture: log collection, normalization, correlation, alerting
Detection engineering: writing Sigma rules and SIEM correlation rules
Threat hunting: hypothesis-driven, IoC-based, anomaly-based approaches
Alert triage: true positive, false positive, benign true positive
MITRE ATT&CK for detection coverage mapping
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing Sigma rules to detect lateral movement (PsExec, WMI) and importing them into Splunk for real-time detection
setup_requirements: Sample attack data for rule testing

review.value: Vendor-agnostic detection rule format. Rules describe the log source, detection logic, and metadata. Can be converted to Splunk SPL, Elastic Query, QRadar AQL, etc.
Proactive search for threats that evade existing detections. Starts with a hypothesis (e.g., 'an attacker is using DNS tunneling'), then searches for evidence to confirm or deny.

learning_objectives: Execute containment actions for different incident types
Balance containment urgency with evidence preservation
Implement network and host-based isolation techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: Containment is the race to limit damage while preserving evidence. The wrong containment action can alert the attacker or destroy critical forensic artifacts.
key_concepts: Short-term containment: network isolation, account lockout, DNS sinkhole
Long-term containment: clean system rebuild, enhanced monitoring
Containment for ransomware: isolate, assess encryption scope, preserve samples
Evidence preservation during containment: memory capture before shutdown
Communication during containment: stakeholders, legal, PR
lecture_notes.demonstration: Executing a containment workflow: isolating a compromised host via switch port shutdown, capturing memory, and sinkholing a C2 domain

review.value: Redirecting malware C2 domain queries to an internal IP. Prevents C2 communication without alerting the attacker that the host has been detected (unlike network isolation).
Always capture volatile evidence (memory, running processes) before containment actions that may destroy it (e.g., powering off a system, killing processes).

learning_objectives: Plan and execute threat eradication across compromised systems
Develop a recovery plan with verification milestones
Integrate incident response with business continuity planning

lecture_notes.introduction: Eradication removes the threat completely, and recovery restores business operations. Both must be thorough: incomplete eradication leads to reinfection.
key_concepts: Eradication checklist: malware removal, backdoor identification, credential reset
Clean rebuild vs remediation decision framework
Recovery sequence: critical systems first, with verification
Monitoring during recovery: detecting persistent access
Business continuity: RPO, RTO, and communication plans
lecture_notes.demonstration: Executing a structured eradication and recovery for a simulated ransomware incident, including credential rotation and backup verification
setup_requirements: Compromised lab systems from previous exercises

review.value: When in doubt, rebuild from known-good media rather than attempting to clean a compromised system. Attackers plant multiple persistence mechanisms; missing even one means reinfection.
Recovery Point Objective: maximum acceptable data loss (determines backup frequency). Recovery Time Objective: maximum acceptable downtime (determines infrastructure investment).

learning_objectives: Execute a structured response to a ransomware incident
Assess decryption options and payment considerations

lecture_notes.introduction: Ransomware incidents are the most common crisis cybersecurity teams face today. A well-practiced response plan can mean the difference between recovery in days versus months.
key_concepts: Ransomware IR workflow: detect, isolate, assess scope, identify variant
Decryption options: NoMoreRansom.org, known decryptors, backup restoration
Payment decision framework: legal, ethical, and practical considerations
Ransomware-specific eradication: identifying initial access and persistence
Post-ransomware hardening: preventing reinfection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Responding to a simulated Conti-style ransomware incident: network isolation, scope assessment, backup integrity check, and recovery execution

review.value: FBI advises against payment as it funds criminal enterprises and does not guarantee recovery. OFAC sanctions may make payment illegal if the group is sanctioned. Always consult legal counsel.
Determine which systems are encrypted, which are accessible but potentially compromised, and which are confirmed clean. This drives recovery prioritization.

learning_objectives: Produce incident reports for different stakeholders
Derive actionable improvements from incident lessons learned

lecture_notes.introduction: The post-incident phase is where organizations learn and improve. Without structured lessons learned, the same types of incidents recur.
key_concepts: Blameless postmortem methodology
Incident report structure: timeline, root cause, contributing factors, action items
Metrics: MTTD (Mean Time to Detect), MTTC (Mean Time to Contain), MTTR (Mean Time to Recover)
Action item tracking and verification
Detection and prevention gap analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a blameless postmortem for the simulated incidents from previous weeks, deriving specific detection and prevention improvements

review.value: Focus on systemic failures, not individual blame. Ask 'what controls failed?' not 'who failed?'. This encourages honest reporting and identifies root causes.
MTTD measures detection capability, MTTC measures containment speed, MTTR measures recovery efficiency. Track trends over time to demonstrate improvement.

questions.question: At 2:00 AM, your SIEM alerts on unusual PowerShell activity from a finance workstation. The user is not typically active at this hour. The command includes 'Invoke-WebRequest' to an external IP. Walk through your first 30 minutes of incident response, including specific actions and their order.
questions.answer: Minutes 0-5: Validate the alert in SIEM. Check if the user account shows a legitimate logon (Event 4624) or if credentials may be compromised. Query EDR for the full PowerShell command. Minutes 5-10: Declare an incident and classify severity (likely High given off-hours activity with external communication). Notify the IR lead. Minutes 10-15: Short-term containment: block the external IP at the firewall to sever potential C2. Do NOT isolate the host yet (preserve volatile evidence). Minutes 15-25: Capture memory remotely using EDR capability or WinPMEM. Collect PowerShell script block logs (Event ID 4104). Check if Invoke-WebRequest downloaded a payload and if it executed. Minutes 25-30: Assess scope: query SIEM for other hosts communicating with the same external IP. Check if the finance user's credentials were used elsewhere. Begin timeline documentation.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a DNS sinkhole and network isolation as containment techniques. For each, describe a scenario where it would be the preferred approach and why.
questions.answer: DNS sinkhole redirects malware C2 domain queries to an internal server, severing C2 communication while the host remains on the network. Preferred when: you have identified multiple infected hosts communicating with the same C2 domain and need to contain all of them simultaneously without disrupting business operations, or when you want to monitor which additional hosts attempt C2 communication. Network isolation physically or logically disconnects the host from the network entirely. Preferred when: the attacker has direct IP-based C2 (DNS sinkhole is ineffective), the host is actively exfiltrating large volumes of data, or the attacker has demonstrated ability to use alternative C2 channels. Key tradeoff: DNS sinkhole is stealthier and less disruptive but only works for DNS-resolved C2; network isolation is comprehensive but alerts the attacker and disrupts business.

questions.question: Your organization was hit by ransomware. 200 of 500 workstations and 3 of 10 servers are encrypted. Your most recent full backup is 48 hours old, and the incremental backup from 12 hours ago may be compromised. Describe your recovery decision framework and the specific steps to safely restore operations.
questions.answer: Decision framework: (1) Verify backup integrity: restore the 48-hour-old full backup to an isolated environment and scan for ransomware indicators. Test the 12-hour incremental by restoring and scanning in isolation. (2) Assess acceptable data loss: 48 hours of data loss may be acceptable if the incremental is compromised. (3) Recovery sequence: restore critical servers first (Active Directory, email, ERP) from verified clean backups to rebuilt/reformatted hardware. (4) Workstation strategy: reimage all 500 workstations (not just the 200 encrypted) since unencrypted ones may have dormant malware. Use a known-good golden image. (5) Credential rotation: reset ALL passwords (domain admin first, then all users) before reconnecting restored systems. (6) Enhanced monitoring: deploy EDR with heightened alerting on all restored systems. (7) Staged reconnection: bring systems online in groups, monitoring for reinfection indicators.

rubric.key: Comprehensive recovery approach including unencrypted machines
Credential rotation and monitoring during recovery

questions.question: After resolving a major security incident, you need to conduct a post-incident review. The CISO wants metrics to present to the board. Describe the specific metrics you would calculate, how you would calculate them, and what each metric tells the board about the organization's security posture.
questions.answer: Metrics: (1) MTTD (Mean Time to Detect): time from initial compromise to first detection alert. Calculated by subtracting the earliest evidence of compromise (from forensic timeline) from the first SIEM alert timestamp. Tells the board how quickly threats are detected. (2) MTTC (Mean Time to Contain): time from detection to effective containment. Calculated from first alert to confirmed isolation of all affected systems. Tells the board how quickly damage is limited. (3) MTTR (Mean Time to Recover): time from containment to full business operations restoration. Tells the board about organizational resilience. (4) Scope metrics: percentage of systems compromised, data volume potentially exposed. Tells the board about blast radius. (5) Cost metrics: direct costs (IR consulting, overtime, hardware) and indirect costs (lost revenue, reputational impact). Tells the board about financial impact for risk quantification.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of what each metric communicates

major_assignments.title: Incident Response Tabletop and Plan Development
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive incident response plan for a fictional organization, then execute it in a graded tabletop exercise against a multi-phase attack scenario injected by the instructor.
objectives: Develop a comprehensive incident response plan
Demonstrate effective incident handling in a tabletop exercise
Produce an incident report and post-incident review
Identify detection and response gaps and propose improvements
deliverables: Post-incident report with timeline and findings
Lessons learned document with actionable improvements

criteria: Complete plan covering all NIST IR phases
Clear roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths
Communication templates and stakeholder notification procedures
Integration with business continuity
Appropriate triage and severity classification
Effective containment decisions balancing speed and evidence
Logical eradication and recovery sequence
Clear communication and decision documentation
Accurate timeline of events and response actions
Root cause analysis with evidence
Impact assessment
Professional report formatting
Specific gaps identified in detection, containment, and recovery
Actionable improvement recommendations
Metrics-based assessment of response performance
Blameless analysis focused on systemic improvements
Professional-quality incident response plan ready for organizational adoption
Effective performance in tabletop exercise demonstrating sound judgment
Thorough incident report with accurate timeline and root cause
Actionable lessons learned that would measurably improve future response

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of incident response concepts, frameworks, and decision-making

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on incident detection, containment, and recovery exercises

major_assignments.description: IR plan development and tabletop exercise

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all incident response and management topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and tabletop exercises. Missed tabletop exercises cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Tabletop exercises are team-based. IR plans and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All incident reports must be based on actual exercise observations. Fabricating timeline entries or findings is grounds for course failure.
course_policies.lab_safety: All incident response exercises must be conducted within the designated lab environment. Simulated attacks must not target production systems.
